ground_truth: Topic: Our unit on language variation looks at how different dialects of English can differ in parts of their mental grammar (phonology, morphology, syntax, etc.) in systematic ways. Write a paper about systematic differences between Standard American English (SAE) and another dialect of English. The paper should include (i) what SAE and the other dialect are (e.g., who speaks it, where it is spoken, etc.), (ii) explanation of the phenomenon you are investigating (e.g., the auxiliary system, inflectional morphology, etc.), and (iii) similarities and differences between the two dialects with respect to that phenomenon. Requirements: 4-5 pages in length. The length does not include references, but please include references in your draft. Double-spaced, 1-inch margins, 12pt-font: Calibri or Times New Roman. At least 3 references to academic sources including at least one that was not assigned for class. APA style references/citations. All work must be your own. Timeline and submission: Topic: We will discuss in class on Monday, 2/9. Rough draft (at least 2.5 pages): due at the beginning of class on Wednesday, 2/18. Remember this draft will not be graded, but your grade on the final draft will take into account whether and how you incorporated the feedback on it. Peer review: due at the beginning of class on Wednesday, 2/25 (HuskyCT). Final draft: due with cover letter at 11:59pm on Wednesday, 3/4 (HuskyCT or hard copy). Additional info: I cannot give extensions for the rough draft or peer review. If you need an extension for the final draft, you must speak with me ahead of time. I highly encourage you to come to office hours to discuss your paper topic, go over ideas, drafts, etc. Extra credit (1pt): Alongside your paper, write and submit an annotated bibliography of your references. An annotated bibliography should include a summary and evaluation of each of your sources and address how the source fits into your research and writing. Common questions addressed by annotated bibliography include but are not limited to: Was this source helpful to you? How did you use this source in your project? How does it help you shape your argument? Has it changed how you think about your topic? The extra credit writing is due together with the final draft (11:59pm, 3/4).
